figures.caption: Table 5. Comparison with state-of-the-art TTA methods on CIFAR10-C, CIFAR100-C, and ImageNet-C (severity level 5). Bold numbers indicate the best results in each column.
Table 6. Comparison with state-of-the-art TTA methods on small batch size regime (batch size =4). Reported are average classification errors (%), averaged over 3 runs. Bests are in bold.
Table 7. Error rate comparison (%) on CIFAR100-C corruptions over time under the continual test-time adaptation setting. Bold numbers indicate the best results in each column.

Figure 1. Visualization of the adaptive activation function. (a) The base activation functions ϕ(x). (b) The AcTTA activation output g(x); each parameter set [ ·, ·, · ] denotes [λ+, λ−, c]. (c) The first derivative g′(x) with varying (λ+, λ−) (d) The effect of the shift parameter c, which avoids strict zero-centering during adaptation.

Figure 2. Visualizations of adapted activations during AcTTA. AcTTA-ReLU results are obtained from WRN-28 on CIFAR10C after completing adaptation on the Gaussian noise corruption. AcTTA-GELU results are from ViT-B/16 on ImageNet-C at the same adaptation endpoint. The learned activation exhibits flexible, channel-specific shaping behavior, adapting its form according to the input distribution. The three legend values denote the adapted parameter values of AcTTA, [λ+, λ−, c], corresponding to positive/negative slope modulation and shift.

Table 2. Ablation on trainable parameters of AcTTA at test time (λneg, λpos, and c) within the Tent [27] framework. Reported is the error rate on CIFAR10-C (WRN-28) and ImageNet-C (ViT-B/16). AcTTA∗ denotes the variant using a learning rate scaled by ×10 (as defined in Table 1). Batch size is 128.
